title: David Birney

infobox.value: David Edwin Birney  (1939-04-23)April 23, 1939  [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), U.S.

[Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") ([B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts"), English) [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") ([M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"), Theatre Arts)

(April 23, 1939 – April 27, 2022) was an American actor and director whose career included performances in both contemporary and classical roles in theatre, film, and television. He is noted for having played the title role in the television series *[Serpico](/wiki/Serpico_(TV_series) "Serpico (TV series)")*. He also starred in *[Bridget Loves Bernie](/wiki/Bridget_Loves_Bernie "Bridget Loves Bernie")*, an early 1970s TV series about an interfaith marriage that also starred [Meredith Baxter](/wiki/Meredith_Baxter "Meredith Baxter"), whom he married after the series ended. He also portrayed Dr. Ben Samuels in *[St. Elsewhere](/wiki/St._Elsewhere "St. Elsewhere")* from 1982 until 1983.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor and director (1939–2022)

For the American Civil War general, see [David B. Birney](/wiki/David_B._Birney "David B. Birney"). For the Episcopal bishop, see [David Birney (bishop)](/wiki/David_Birney_(bishop) "David Birney (bishop)").

| David Birney | |

| Died | April 27, 2022(2022-04-27) (aged 83)  [Santa Monica, California](/wiki/Santa_Monica,_California "Santa Monica, California"), U.S. |
| Education | [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") ([B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts"), English) [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") ([M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"), Theatre Arts) |

**David Edwin Birney** (April 23, 1939 – April 27, 2022) was an American actor and director whose career included performances in both contemporary and classical roles in theatre, film, and television. He is noted for having played the title role in the television series *[Serpico](/wiki/Serpico_(TV_series) "Serpico (TV series)")*. He also starred in *[Bridget Loves Bernie](/wiki/Bridget_Loves_Bernie "Bridget Loves Bernie")*, an early 1970s TV series about an interfaith marriage that also starred [Meredith Baxter](/wiki/Meredith_Baxter "Meredith Baxter"), whom he married after the series ended. He also portrayed Dr. Ben Samuels in *[St. Elsewhere](/wiki/St._Elsewhere "St. Elsewhere")* from 1982 until 1983.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Birney was born to an Irish Catholic family in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), on April 23, 1939. His father, Edwin, worked as a special agent for the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"); his mother, Jeanne (née McGee), was a housewife before becoming a real estate agent.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) Birney attended schools in [Brooklyn, Ohio](/wiki/Brooklyn,_Ohio "Brooklyn, Ohio"), and graduated from West High School in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"). Named to the [National Honor Society](/wiki/National_Honor_Society "National Honor Society"), he lettered in basketball, football, and track. He held a [B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") degree from [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") with "High Distinction" in English literature, English Honors. At the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), Birney earned an [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") in theatre arts,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) acting and directing, studying with Ralph Freud and William Melnitz. He held a teaching assistant fellowship and was awarded an honorary [Ph.D.](/wiki/Ph.D. "Ph.D.") in Humanities from [Southern Utah University](/wiki/Southern_Utah_University "Southern Utah University").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stage")]

While in the [U.S. Army](/wiki/U.S._Army "U.S. Army"), Birney won an All Army Entertainment contest and received the "[Barter Theatre](/wiki/Barter_Theatre "Barter Theatre") Award" in 1965. Since the award was an equity contract with the Company for an entire season, he consequently spent the next season with the [Barter Theatre](/wiki/Barter_Theatre "Barter Theatre"), the State Theatre of Virginia, starring or appearing[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) in fifteen shows, directing two others. In the following two years he went on to perform with a range of companies and productions, [off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") and in several regional repertory theatres. His New York debut was with [Joe Papp](/wiki/Joseph_Papp "Joseph Papp")'s [New York Shakespeare Festival](/wiki/New_York_Shakespeare_Festival "New York Shakespeare Festival") as Antipholus of Syracuse in [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")’s *[The Comedy of Errors](/wiki/The_Comedy_of_Errors "The Comedy of Errors")*.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1)

Birney worked continually in the theatre performing leading roles with some of the most important theatres in the country. His stage credits included starring roles on Broadway in *[Amadeus](/wiki/Amadeus_(play) "Amadeus (play)")*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) *[Benefactors](/wiki/Benefactors_(play) "Benefactors (play)")*,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and *[Man and Superman](/wiki/Man_and_Superman "Man and Superman")*.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He also had major roles at the American Shakespeare Festival,[[6]](#cite_note-Gussow-6) New York's Lincoln Center Repertory Theatre,[[7]](#cite_note-7) the New York Shakespeare Festival,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) Los Angeles' [Mark Taper Forum](/wiki/Mark_Taper_Forum "Mark Taper Forum"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) Washington, D.C.'s Shakespeare Theatre,[[9]](#cite_note-9) Princeton's [McCarter Theatre](/wiki/McCarter_Theatre "McCarter Theatre"),[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) the [Great Lakes Shakespeare Festival](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Theater "Great Lakes Theater"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and numerous regional theatres around the country.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1)

Representative roles included: [Prince Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet"),[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) [Macbeth](/wiki/Macbeth "Macbeth"),[[11]](#cite_note-Herman-11) Romeo and Mercutio in *[Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet")*,[[6]](#cite_note-Gussow-6)[[11]](#cite_note-Herman-11) [Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_(play) "Richard II (play)"), [Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_(play) "Richard III (play)"),[[11]](#cite_note-Herman-11) Benedick in *[Much Ado About Nothing](/wiki/Much_Ado_About_Nothing "Much Ado About Nothing")*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) Shylock in *[The Merchant of Venice](/wiki/The_Merchant_of_Venice "The Merchant of Venice")*,[[12]](#cite_note-12) Orsino in *[Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night "Twelfth Night")*,[[13]](#cite_note-13) Atticus Finch in *[To Kill a Mockingbird](/wiki/To_Kill_a_Mockingbird "To Kill a Mockingbird")*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) Jack Tanner in *Man and Superman*,[[11]](#cite_note-Herman-11) Christy Mahon in *[The Playboy of the Western World](/wiki/The_Playboy_of_the_Western_World "The Playboy of the Western World")*,[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) Young Man in *Summertree*,[[16]](#cite_note-16) Cusins in *[Major Barbara](/wiki/Major_Barbara "Major Barbara")*,[[17]](#cite_note-17) Jerry in *[The Zoo Story](/wiki/The_Zoo_Story "The Zoo Story")*, Algernon in *[The Importance of Being Earnest](/wiki/The_Importance_of_Being_Earnest "The Importance of Being Earnest")*,[[18]](#cite_note-concord-18) Arthur in *[Camelot](/wiki/Camelot_(musical) "Camelot (musical)")*,[[19]](#cite_note-19) Higgins in *[My Fair Lady](/wiki/My_Fair_Lady "My Fair Lady")*,[[20]](#cite_note-20) Matt Friedman in *[Talley's Folly](/wiki/Talley%27s_Folly "Talley's Folly")*,[[21]](#cite_note-21) David in *[Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(play) "Social Security (play)")*,[[22]](#cite_note-22) Andrew in *[Love Letters](/wiki/Love_Letters_(play) "Love Letters (play)")*,[[18]](#cite_note-concord-18) Jamie in *[A Moon for the Misbegotten](/wiki/A_Moon_for_the_Misbegotten "A Moon for the Misbegotten")*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) Victor in *[The Price](/wiki/The_Price_(play) "The Price (play)")*,[[18]](#cite_note-concord-18) Jaques in *[As You Like It](/wiki/As_You_Like_It "As You Like It")*,[[23]](#cite_note-23) the Dauphin in *[King John](/wiki/King_John_(play) "King John (play)")*,[[11]](#cite_note-Herman-11) and Shaw in [*Dear Liar*](https://www.ibdb.com/broadway-production/dear-liar-2194).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Audiobooks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Audiobooks")]

Birney recorded numerous [audiobook](/wiki/Audiobook "Audiobook") bestsellers, including works by [Dean Koontz](/wiki/Dean_Koontz "Dean Koontz"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Paul Theroux](/wiki/Paul_Theroux "Paul Theroux"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Annie Dillard](/wiki/Annie_Dillard "Annie Dillard"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) and [Orson Scott Card](/wiki/Orson_Scott_Card "Orson Scott Card").[[28]](#cite_note-28) He was conferred the [Audie Award](/wiki/Audie_Awards "Audie Awards") for his reading of Julie Salomon's *The Christmas Tree*, and was also bestowed several *AudioFile Magazine* Earphone Awards.[[29]](#cite_note-29) He played [Anakin Skywalker](/wiki/Darth_Vader "Darth Vader") in the [radio adaptation](/wiki/Star_Wars_(radio_series) "Star Wars (radio series)") of *[Return of the Jedi](/wiki/Return_of_the_Jedi "Return of the Jedi")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television")]

Aside from his title role in *Bridget Loves Bernie*, Birney appeared frequently on television,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) building a career in TV movies, regular series and [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries"). He starred in such series as *[Live Shot](/wiki/Live_Shot "Live Shot")*, *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")*,[[32]](#cite_note-Rotten_Tomatoes-32) *[St. Elsewhere](/wiki/St._Elsewhere "St. Elsewhere")*, *[The Adams Chronicles](/wiki/The_Adams_Chronicles "The Adams Chronicles")*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) *[Glitter](/wiki/Glitter_(American_TV_series) "Glitter (American TV series)")*,[[33]](#cite_note-33) *Serpico*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1)[[34]](#cite_note-Holsapple-34) *[Fantasy Island](/wiki/Fantasy_Island "Fantasy Island")*, *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*, *[McMillan & Wife](/wiki/McMillan_%26_Wife "McMillan & Wife")*,[[32]](#cite_note-Rotten_Tomatoes-32) *[The F.B.I.](/wiki/The_F.B.I._(TV_series) "The F.B.I. (TV series)")*,[[35]](#cite_note-TVG_Filmography-35) and *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*.[[36]](#cite_note-36) He also starred in the episode "The Nomads" from the 1977 series *[Quinn Martin's Tales of the Unexpected](/wiki/Quinn_Martin%27s_Tales_of_the_Unexpected "Quinn Martin's Tales of the Unexpected")*, known in the United Kingdom as *Twist in the Tale*.[[37]](#cite_note-37) His miniseries credits include *[Testimony of Two Men](/w/index.php?title=Testimony_of_Two_Men&action=edit&redlink=1 "Testimony of Two Men (page does not exist)")*, *[Valley of the Dolls](/wiki/Jacqueline_Susann%27s_Valley_of_the_Dolls "Jacqueline Susann's Valley of the Dolls")*, *[Night of the Fox](/wiki/Night_of_the_Fox "Night of the Fox")*, and *[Master of the Game](/wiki/Master_of_the_Game_(novel)#Adaptations "Master of the Game (novel)")*.[[38]](#cite_note-TCM-38) He also appeared in the TV series the Love Boat with his wife where he played an Italian escort to an American woman whose friend arranged the encounter.

Birney also appeared in leading roles in many television films, including *[Long Journey Home](/wiki/The_Irish_in_America:_Long_Journey_Home "The Irish in America: Long Journey Home")* and *[The Deadly Game](/wiki/Serpico_(TV_series) "Serpico (TV series)")*.[[34]](#cite_note-Holsapple-34)[[38]](#cite_note-TCM-38) He also appeared in the soap operas *[The Best of Everything](/wiki/The_Best_of_Everything_(TV_series) "The Best of Everything (TV series)")* and *[Love Is a Many Splendored Thing](/wiki/Love_Is_a_Many_Splendored_Thing_(TV_series) "Love Is a Many Splendored Thing (TV series)")*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Writing and directing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Writing and directing")]

Birney edited and adapted for the stage a two-character play based on some of [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain")’s shorter works and letters. The piece, *Mark Twain's The Diaries of Adam and Eve*, was presented on the [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") series *[American Playhouse](/wiki/American_Playhouse "American Playhouse")*.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Developing the play subsequently for the stage, Birney directed and starred in productions for regional theatres such as the [Hartford Stage](/wiki/Hartford_Stage "Hartford Stage") (opening the Mark Twain Festival in Hartford), the [Capital Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Capital_Repertory_Theatre "Capital Repertory Theatre"), and on tour in performing arts centers across the country. A second play, *A Christmas Pudding*, a Christmas collage of song, story and poetry of the season was published by [Samuel French, Inc.](/wiki/Samuel_French,_Inc. "Samuel French, Inc.")[[18]](#cite_note-concord-18)

### Professional associations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Professional associations")]

Birney served on the Large Theatre Panel of the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts") and was a board member of the [Foundation for Biomedical Research](/wiki/Foundation_for_Biomedical_Research "Foundation for Biomedical Research"). He also served on the Theatre and Dance Panel of the Jacob Javits Fellowship Foundation. For Dartmouth College he served as a member of the Board of Overseers for the [Hopkins Center for the Arts](/wiki/Hopkins_Center_for_the_Arts "Hopkins Center for the Arts"). He initiated and chaired the Class of '61 Legacy: The American Tradition in Performance, helping to create a substantial endowment dedicated to live performance at the Hopkins Center at Dartmouth College.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

For five years, Birney co-chaired the [American Diabetes Association](/wiki/American_Diabetes_Association "American Diabetes Association"), speaking and fund raising for the Association. He was an advisor for the Children’s Rights Council, a national nonprofit organization advocating access to both parents after divorce or separation. His contribution to classical theatre was recognized with Washington's Shakespeare Theatre's Millennium Award.[[18]](#cite_note-concord-18)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Birney married actress [Meredith Baxter](/wiki/Meredith_Baxter "Meredith Baxter") in 1974. They had starred together in the 1972–73 TV series *[Bridget Loves Bernie](/wiki/Bridget_Loves_Bernie "Bridget Loves Bernie")*. During their marriage, she was known as Meredith Baxter Birney. Together, they had three children: Kate, Mollie, and Peter. They divorced in 1989. In 2011, she said Birney emotionally and physically abused her during their marriage.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1)[[42]](#cite_note-42) He published a lengthy statement on his website disputing the allegations.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Death")]

In the December 2017 edition of the *Wide Wide World* newsletter for [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") class of 1961 alumni, it was disclosed that Birney had been diagnosed with [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Birney died on April 27, 2022, four days after his 83rd birthday, at his home in [Santa Monica, California](/wiki/Santa_Monica,_California "Santa Monica, California").[[1]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-1) At the time of his death, he was in a domestic partnership with Michele Roberge.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Filmography")]

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Birney&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Film")]

* *[Caravan to Vaccarès](/wiki/Caravan_to_Vaccar%C3%A8s_(film) "Caravan to Vaccarès (film)")* (1974)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[32]](#cite_note-Rotten_Tomatoes-32)[[38]](#cite_note-TCM-38) – Bowman
* *[Trial by Combat](/wiki/Trial_by_Combat "Trial by Combat")* (1976)[[48]](#cite_note-AFI-48)[[49]](#cite_note-BFI-49) – Sir John Gifford
